evidence: A peer-to-peer strategic conversation between Vrijen Attawar (founder of Careerspan) and Chris Hanson (special education teacher turned edtech founder)

source_display: AI as Brain Crutch for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching

source_display: Pilot Trap / Death by Thousand Cuts
evidence: Vrijen shared advice from his edtech experience: avoid the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is
He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly
Chris Hanson (special education teacher turned edtech founder)... building his edtech product for almost a year
Chris is building an ADHD support product called '360'
Vrijen invited Chris to join the Future of Higher Education group (6,000 members globally, ~1,000 in NYC, ~1,000 in Boston), where Vrijen serves as NYC co-lead
A reconnection meeting between Vrijen (Careerspan founder) and Maximilian (future simulation product founder)
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing
The consensus is to aim for a sale mid-year but spend the next few months generating revenue first, which Vrijen believes will massively improve their valuation
He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value - passing an algorithm assessment doesn't prove someone can deliver value to the company

The conversation critiqued abstracted roles like product owner and project manager, suggesting these roles exist primarily to organize attention but don't add direct value to products. Maximilian argued these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape

Maximilian connected this to recent engineering layoffs, observing that engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable. Those accepting tickets from queues without touching product understanding were let go

Vrijen used the example of parents obsessing over SATs because numbers are concrete and discrete, but noted this flattens humans to two axes inappropriately. The same person could be a generational hire for one role and a disaster for another, demonstrating context-dependent value
Maximilian (future simulation product founder)... His product enables users to fail safely and restart at the click of a button

He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs. He outlined three strategic levels of context engineering
Three-level framework: Conversation (tactical), Environment (persistent context), Pipeline (external data integration) - mastering all three creates an emergent personal AI operating system
Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems
embrace the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team—recognizing the AI is capable of more than you initially realize

source_display: Plan Files as Future of Coding
evidence: He emphasized the importance of intentional prompting—documenting AI conversations in plan files that mix prose and code in stepwise fashion. He positioned this as the future of coding: planning systems through narrative structure rather than pure code alone
job security is dead and you need to be building career security for yourself... it has a lot to do with being findable and being visible

Logan Currie demonstrated deep analysis of 18 years of LinkedIn data using Claude Code, having exported 48 CSV files containing 5,000+ messages, connections, search history, and activity data. Key discoveries included: Network mismatch - Only 3,300 LinkedIn connections versus 45,000 imported contacts
Posting correlation: Documented 1,800% increase in inbound connections when increasing posting frequency, strong evidence for visibility strategies
An AI productivity workshop presented by Careerspan (Vrijen Attawar and Logan Currie) and Zo Computer (Ben Guo)
An AI productivity workshop presented by Careerspan (Vrijen Attawar and Logan Currie) and Zo Computer (Ben Guo)
Ben Guo introduced Zo Computer as a managed version of Claude Code, giving the AI its own computer (managed cloud server) while handling security and infrastructure complexity

He shared his metacognitive patterns for working effectively with AI: narrative before data, use yourself as a guinea pig, never accept the first answer, consistently ask 'what else?' and 'delight me'
